pred_reason: SWC-101 is applicable due to the reentrancy vulnerability in _transfer function violating checks-effects-interactions pattern. SWC-107 is justified by the weak PRNG in approve function relying on block timestamp for critical logic. Evidence: _transfer emits Transfer event before updating balances (Finding 1); approve uses require(now % 10000000 == 0) for critical condition (Finding 4); Static analysis confirmed weak-prng vulnerability (Finding 4)

findings.Description: The _transfer function violates checks-effects-interactions pattern by emitting Transfer event (which can trigger external calls for contract recipients) before updating balances. This vulnerability appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Attackers can perform reentrancy attacks to drain funds from the contract before balance updates are completed.
findings.Location: _transfer function in AkitaRyu contract (lines 264-275)

findings.Description: Multiple arithmetic operations bypass SafeMath protection, including raw multiplication in decreaseAllowance and uncontrolled supply increases. This vulnerability appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Integer overflow/underflow can occur, allowing token supply manipulation and potential fund loss.
findings.Location: decreaseAllowance function (line 164: rTotal = amount * 10**18;) and increaseAllowance function (lines 219-224)

findings.Description: Owner can mint unlimited tokens through increaseAllowance function without any supply cap. This vulnerability appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Complete token supply inflation, devaluing all existing holdings to near zero.
findings.Location: increaseAllowance function (lines 219-224)

findings.Description: The approve function uses a weak pseudo-random number generator based on block timestamp. This vulnerability appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Predictable approval timing enables front-running attacks and manipulation of approval transactions.
findings.Location: approve function (line 262: require(now % 10000000 == 0);) and static analysis finding [High] weak-prng

findings.Issue: Missing Access Controls on Critical Functions
findings.Description: Critical state-changing functions like decreaseAllowance and increaseAllowance lack proper access control modifiers. This vulnerability appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Any user can manipulate critical contract parameters and mint unlimited tokens to themselves.
findings.Location: decreaseAllowance and increaseAllowance functions (missing onlyOwner modifier)

findings.Description: The approve function uses a strict equality check with timestamp modulo operation. This vulnerability appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Timing-based condition creates unpredictable approval behavior and potential transaction failures.
findings.Location: approve function (line 262) and static analysis finding [Medium] incorrect-equality

findings.Description: Critical address setting functions lack zero address validation. This vulnerability appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Accidental setting of critical addresses to zero address can break contract functionality.
findings.Location: Approve and setrouteChain functions, static analysis findings [Low] missing-zero-check

findings.Description: The _transfer function contains a flawed restriction that can be easily bypassed through transaction splitting. This vulnerability appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Intended transfer limits to router address are completely ineffective, allowing unlimited transfers.
findings.Location: _transfer function conditional check (lines 268-270)

findings.Description: increaseAllowance and decreaseAllowance functions implement non-standard minting/burning logic instead of ERC20 allowance management. This vulnerability appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Breaks ERC20 standard compliance and creates confusion about function behavior.
findings.Location: increaseAllowance and decreaseAllowance functions (lines 166-168, 177-182)

findings.Description: Critical state changes like rTotal modification lack event emissions. This vulnerability appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Reduced transparency and difficulty in tracking contract state changes.
findings.Location: decreaseAllowance function and static analysis finding [Low] events-maths

findings.Description: The approve function relies on block timestamp for critical logic. This vulnerability appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Vulnerable to miner manipulation of block timestamps.
findings.Location: approve function (line 262) and static analysis finding [Low] timestamp
